[Slide 17]
What is Business Development?
"Business development is sales," salespeople will say.
"Business development is growth," some will say, aggressively.
"Business development is new products," marketeers will say.
"Business development is new markets," some will say, concisely.
"Business development is partnerships," others will say, vaguely.
"Business development is hustling," the startup folks will say, evasively.

[Slide 18]
Business development is the creation of long-term value for an organization from customers, markets, and relationships.
Business Development

Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)
Customer lifetime value is the net profit earned from sales to a given customer during the time that customer purchases from the sales organization
CLV is about how the customer is treated over time
Lifetime value is a measure of customer loyalty

[Slide 49]
"It's hard to fill the funnel if it's not under the faucet"
B. Roberson, "What Really Works"
The Sales Funnel

[Slide 50]
Type C / Type B / Type A
Geometric shape / Size of funnel (number of phases) / Velocity of each stage (speed between phases) / Efficiency (cost) of each stage / Effectiveness (% of target) of each stage
Beware of the 'close rate' or 'conversion rate'

[Slide 51]
So how do increase our conversion rate?

[Slide 52]
The Sales Pipeline
A sales pipeline is a visual representation of sales prospects and where they are in the purchasing process. Pipelines also provide an overview of a sales rep's account forecast and how close the rep is to making quota, as well as how close a sales team as a whole is to reaching quota.

[Slide 54]
Pace & Space

[Slide 57]
Define Scope / Segment Market / Qualify / Personify / Identify
Identification

[Slide 58]
Steps in the Prospecting Stage
Define Scope / Market Segmentation / Identify Targets / Qualify Targets / Personify the Business

[Slide 59]
Step 1: Define Scope
Creating a Target Profile: CAN Modeling
Characteristics — Revenues, structure, employees, products, customers, markets
Activities — Objectives, competencies, weaknesses, strategies, plans
Needs — Internal and external opportunities, internal and external threats

[Slide 60]
Step 2: Segment Market
Market Segmentation: Organizing a Target Search
Internal & External Segmentation
Internal segment will be the first look: puppy dog principle
Work with Available Knowledge / You can make good decisions while still keeping an eye on ROI
Prioritize CAN Factors / The perfect prospect doesn't exist: draw lines

[Slide 61]
Step 3: Identify Targets
C2 Method
Current Customers — Always the first look / Star customers with highest current utilization / Opportunistic development strategic approach
Customers' Competitors — Maintain integrity of CAN modeling / Provides efficient attention process
Competitors' Customers — Maintain integrity of CAN modeling / Provides efficient qualification process

[Slide 62]
Step 4: Qualify Targets
Ranking the identified targets by overall attractiveness
M — does the target have the money to buy?
A — does the target have the authority to buy?
D — does the target have the desire to buy?
A traditional model
GUT: simplistic, short-sighted, high-risk
FAST: more useful in B2C or diffuse markets

[Slide 63]
Step 5: Personify Targets
Identify the target individual(s) within target companies
This is where traditional sources make sense: referrals / networking / trade organizations
Try some purposeful technology: Google / LinkedIn / Social Media / Databases

[Slide 64]
So how do we increase the top of the funnel?

[Slide 65]
SEO
SEO (Search Engine Optimization) means that buyers are provided content through a search engine, like Google. To optimize search engine results, marketers should focus on publishing content that contains relevant keywords. Think about why a buyer would search for your product – to solve a specific challenge or overcome a pain point? Then tailor keywords on your website and in published content to answer those challenges directly.

[Slide 66]
Content Marketing
Involves the strategic use of information to create a dialogue with a potential customer. Content can be targeted to buyers at different points on their journey: eBooks, guides, and case studies can require contact info to download (lead capture). This gives marketing teams information about the prospect, their industry and their stage in the decision-making process.

[Slide 67]
Paid Advertising
Paid search or pay-per-click (PPC). / Paid social posts. / Programmatic advertising. / Display ads. / Paid sponsorship of a website post or newsletter.

[Slide 68]
Organic Social Media
Social media users number almost 4.2 billion globally; spending an average of 2.5 hours per day on various platforms

[Slide 69]
Account-Based Marketing
Instead of casting a wide net and bringing in leads from any company, account-based marketing (ABM) focuses on specific targets for marketing efforts. While this may decrease the number of leads that a marketing campaign brings in, it increases the relevance, resulting in a better overall ROI.

[Slide 70]
Content Mapping
Taking an inventory of existing content and mapping its place in the customer journey benefits businesses in a few ways. First, a content map helps a marketer understand what content should be offered at each stage of the journey to have the greatest impact. It also helps identify missing pieces of content that would help the buyer down the funnel so that those can be added to an ongoing marketing strategy.

[Slide 71]
Email Nurturing
Nurturing leads through regular emails can be the best tactic to keep people engaged. By sending targeted emails over time to your contacts, you can gradually encourage more engagement. This momentum is critical.
Nurtured leads have a 23% faster sales cycle and produce on average 20% more sales.

[Slide 72]
Specific Sales Force Activities
Site Visits / Happy Hours / Conferences / Associations / Virtual Events

[Slide 73]
A Knowledge Management Framework for Salespeople
Customer / Organization / Personal / Environment
Sales Organization / Product / Price / Sales support / Competitive Position

[Slide 74]
The Customer
Customer Organizational Knowledge — Customer intelligence – the amount we know about the prospect
Customer Personal Knowledge — Hot buttons – topics which an individual is particularly interested
What are some areas of customer knowledge?

[Slide 75]
Competition can be categorized into one of three buckets.
Positioning for Success: Competition
Response: Contain / Create / Close

[Slide 76]
Response: CONTAIN
Common Mistake: ignore competitively weak variables
Invest: moderate communication capital
CONTAIN by measuring importance of variable and size of gap
CONTAIN by clarifying so that perception of gap is narrowed
CONTAIN by compensating so that actual gap is narrowed
competitors likely have room to counter your resources here / be cautious not to pursue a lost cause
Positioning for Success: Competitively Weak

[Slide 77]
Response: CREATE
Common Mistake: remain dormant on neutral variables
Invest: maximum communication capital
CREATE by uncovering specifics of your standing
CREATE by exploring all contingencies of your offering
CREATE by allocating resources to gain competitive strength
competitors may be taking steps to strengthen their position / be cautious not to be myopic
Positioning for Success: Competitively Neutral

[Slide 78]
Response: CLOSE
Common Mistake: belabor competitively strong variables
Invest: minimum communication capital
CLOSE by gaining early agreement on competitive strength
CLOSE by emphasizing business implications of strength
CLOSE by not allocating additional resources here
competitors are attempting to narrow the gap / be cautious not to be complacent
Positioning for Success: Competitively Strong

[Slide 6]
Span of Attention
You must earn your prospect's attention in the first five seconds of an interview
You must continue to earn it in 60-second increments (How can we do this?)
You must constantly be doing or saying something to hold your prospect's attention
We must establish a connection !!!!!

[Slide 8]

[Slide 37]
Cold calling

[Slide 40]
What are they?
Cold calling is the solicitation of a potential customer who had no prior interaction with a salesperson.
Warm calling, on the other hand, is the solicitation of a customer who had previously expressed interest in the company or product.
Why are they difficult?
Not wired to behave this way. / Anxiety and fear of rejection. / People are overwhelmed. / Better marketing approaches. / Filters and spam detectors.
Cold Calls / What do you do when you get a cold call???

[Slide 41]
Start with a good call list / Take the time you need to create a targeted and high value list of prospects / Use a systematic process to create your target list
Research is valuable…up-to-a-point / Profile your prospects and categorize them. / Leverage commonalities / One connection point is enough
Now is not the time to sell your product or service / Focus on the prospects wants/needs and sell the value of a conversation / Few people have time or interest on the initial contact
Fundamental Rules

[Slide 42]
Take 'No' for an answer / Target total number of appointments, not individual yes/no / Understand the primary objections: Time, interest, cost, decision maker
Scrap the script. / Be situational, flexible and conversational / Listen and respond accordingly / This is not a data dump
Minimize expectations – maximize impact. / Not a sales call / Set time expectations / Present insight and value
Fundamental Rules

[Slide 43]
Tips and techniques
Focus all of your questions on your client, not yourself / Plan all of your questions in advance / Don't follow a tight cold calling scripts / Don't overwhelm your prospect during the first meeting / Don't attempt to sell on your first cold call / Keep your prospect relaxed / Find out what benefit will make your prospect buy from you

[Slide 44]
Providing insight
Insight selling is the process of creating and winning sales opportunities and driving change with ideas that matter.
Opportunity insight: Selling a particular idea or strategy a buyer should pursue but might not know about (Knowledge Broker)
Interaction insight: Providing value in the form of creating insights through buyer and seller conversation (Sales Process)

[Slide 45]
Becoming a Knowledge Broker
Educate buyers with new ideas and perspectives:
Best practices / Industry trends / Minimize risk / Drive demand / Maximize ROI / Optimal solution / Create simplicity or efficiency

[Slide 46]
Levels of value and opportunity
Three potential levels: You / Product/service / Company/brand

A Brief Review of Professional Selling: Questioning & Listening

Questioning – A Funnel Approach / Listening – Why it Matters
Today's Topics

Negotiation / Questioning & Listening / Recommendation / Value Proposition / Connection / Agenda / Handling Objections / Transition
UNDERSTANDING

[Slide 8]
How much time do you get in front of the decision maker?

[Slide 9]
Questioning and Listening

[Slide 10]
What 1 thing do you most often need to find out by asking questions?
questioning

[Slide 11]
Some Level of Dissatisfaction

[Slide 12]
Customers Talking 80% / Salesperson Talking 20%
questioning

[Slide 13]
WARNING!!!!!!
Don't spend too much time here and don't fake it.
Wasteful questions do more harm than good.
Customer and prospects want ANSWERS!!!!!!!
questioning

[Slide 14]
Open-end Questions / Closed-end Questions
Types of questions
Broad-Scoped OE / Narrow-Focused OE / Alternative Choice CE / Single Outcome CE

[Slide 15]
Categories of questions

[Slide 16]
START WITH THE END IN MIND!
Stephen Covey: Seven Habits of Highly Effective People

[Slide 18]
Situation — Provide basic information about the customer's situation
Problem — Get the customer concentrating on particular issues
Implication — Help the customer recognize a problem's consequences (Hurt and Rescue)(Damage and Repair)(Pain and Solution)
Need payoff — Directly connect the problem with the value proposition
S.P.I.N. Selling

[Slide 19]
Finding out facts about the buyer's existing situation.
Tell me about your current operation? How do you manage your time and contacts?
Least powerful of the SPIN questions. Negative relationship to success. Most people ask too many.
Eliminate unnecessary Situation Questions by doing your homework in advance.
Situation questions

[Slide 20]
Asking about problems, difficulties or dissatisfactions that the buyer is experiencing with the existing situation.
Have you ever had trouble managing your time or your contacts? Which parts of the system create error?
More powerful than Situation Questions. People ask more Problem Questions as they become experienced at selling.
Think of your products or services in terms of the problems they solve for buyers—not in terms of the details or characteristics that your products possess.
Problem questions

[Slide 21]
Asking about the consequences or effects of a buyer's problems, difficulties, or dissatisfactions.
What effect does that problem have on your productivity? Could that be impeding your ability to develop good relationships with your customers?
The most powerful of all SPIN questions.
These questions are the hardest. Prepare by identifying and understanding the implications of various suspected needs before the call.
Implication questions

[Slide 22]
Asking about the value or usefulness of a proposed solution. They seek the buyer's opinion as to what life would be like if the problem was solved.
How would better time & customer management help you? Would you like to discuss how we can do that for you?
These questions help the buyer understand the benefits of solving the problem.
Use these questions to get buyers to tell you the benefits of your solution.
Need-payoff questions

[Slide 23]
QUESTIONING GAME
Get a partner / One person raise their hand — What are you passionate about? / Other person, two minutes, only questions. / End with a summary statement of what the person said over the course of two minutes.

[Slide 24]
20 Questions [list of animals, famous people, locations, and foods]

[Slide 26]
Signals that you may not be getting the entire story:
Silence / Incomplete response / Several hesitations, umms, maybes, etc. / Changing responses / Frustration / Overall disengagement
When to ask more questions

[Slide 27]
Potential Strategies – Questioning:
Clarifying questions to confirm current status
Narrow-focused questions on other potential concerns
Broad/funnel strategy – Value and Reputation
Broad/funnel strategy – What They 'Desire from a Graduate Program'
Broad/funnel strategy – Understand the Structure of the Program
Broad/funnel strategy – Outcomes they Hope to Achieve
Broad/funnel strategy – The Learning Environment
Broad/funnel strategy – Orientating to the City and University

[Slide 28]
What is your favorite question?
"I work with dozens of sales organizations, and I understand that many companies are facing challenges with their sales force aging out and finding new salespeople, what are the big issues your company is facing right now?"

[Slide 29]
Now…you have all kinds of information about the customer and the problems they face….
Build a summary statement
Summarize what you just learned in the questioning phase. This becomes the foundation for your value proposition.
What I heard you say is…… / To clarify,…… / To sum up our conversation to this point…

[Slide 30]
Developing Question Funnels: Time for some needs discovery. Create two (2) question funnels that you could use during your interaction. You may not know exactly what the challenges are the prospect has, but take a guess based on the buying center you created above.

[Slide 31]
Importance of Listening

[Slide 32]
Lost in Translation…
"There are some days when no matter what I say it feels like I'm far away in another country & whoever is doing the translating has had far too much to drink."

[Slide 33]
What 1 thing distracts you the most from effective listening?

[Slide 34]
On average, we speak at a rate of 125 words per minute; however, we process information at a rate of over 500 words per minute.
So What?

[Slide 35]
We…
SPEAK at a rate of 125-175 words per minute
LISTEN at a rate of 125-250 words per minute
THINK at a rate of 1000-3000 words per minute
85% of what we know we have learned through listening
Less than 2% of professionals have been trained in listening
People generally listen at a 25% comprehension rate
LISTENING

[Slide 36]
Which one best describes your listening style?
Mind Reader / Rehearser / Filterer / Dreamer / Identifier / Corrector / Comparer / Derailer / Sparrer / Placater / Stereotyper / Elaborator

[Slide 37]
If you spend 6 hours a day with prospects and 3 hours listening to them:
Hear about 90 minutes worth / Actually listen to 45 minutes worth / Understand 22.5 minutes of it / Believe only 11 minutes of it / Remember only 5 5/8 minutes of it
160 minutes / 80 minutes / 40 minutes / 20 minutes / 10 minutes / 5 minutes!!!
Todays Classes

[Slide 38]
How well did you listen?
Hometown / Name of my dog / What does my wife teach / Name of student role playing / Name of other student role playing / How many years at Vlerick / What University did I recruit football players?

[Slide 39]
Three Levels of Listening
Marginal — Recipients hear the words but are easily distracted and may allow their minds to wander
Evaluative — The listener is concentrating on what is being said but is not perceptive in their processing because they focus on literal meaning
Active listening — The listener receives messages, processes them, and responds so as to encourage further communication
Active-empathetic listening takes active listening to a higher level

[Slide 40]
Sensing (Marginal) / Active (Responding) / Processing (Evaluative)
Verbal & Non-verbal

Allow you to clear up any misunderstandings / Allow you to prioritize what is important / Allow you to solidify your credibility

OBJECTIONS

[Slide 23]
Stoppers are genuine objections to all or part of the salesperson's proposal
Stalls are invalid objections (i.e., excuses)
Searches are requests for more information either from the salesperson, the competition, or both
Types of Objections

[Slide 24]
Negotiation / Questioning & Listening / Recommendation / Value Proposition / Connection / Agenda / Handling Objections / Transition
RECOMMENDING

[Slide 25]
Overcoming Objections — Steps in the Process:
Listen very carefully to the prospect's objection / Clarify the concern / Classify the objection to determine when and how to answer it / Cushion the objection (Empathy) / Answer the objection with concern, conviction, and enthusiasm / Follow-up to ensure it is no longer a concern

[Slide 26]
Objection-handling Techniques
Forestall the objection – anticipate in presentation
Compensate – offer something in its place
Counter – 'perhaps we can look at it this way'
Boomerang – 'make the objection the reason to buy'
Feel, felt, found – 'I understand how you feel….other people have felt the same way until they found that the…..'

[Slide 27]
Common Objections
Price / Cost / Quality / Safety / Where to live / Brand / Language / Visa / Job after graduation / No work experience / Don't want to leave home / Significant other / Stay at their current school / Wait until after Pandemic, Ukraine, Economy, etc…… / Work for a couple of years / Applicable knowledge for outside of Belgium/Europe / Energy concerns

[Slide 29]
The recommendation spectrum: nuanced wording is key to success
offer / suggestion / proposal / prescription
Variables for consideration: relationship / credibility / proposal dynamic / product fit / customer type / social styles / industry norms / confidence / instinct

[Slide 28]
The 5 Elements: Main Character
There's a boy who lives on a sandy planet and an old man shows up asking the boy to rescue a princess. He offers the boy a gift: a lightsaber that glows green and can cut down enemies.

[Slide 29]
The 5 Elements: Why now?
The boy demurs until the bad guy's forces destroy the boy's home, killing his family.

[Slide 30]
The 5 Elements: Promised Land
Now he goes with the old man to rescue the princess and stop the bad guy.

[Slide 31]
The 5 Elements: Obstacles & Gifts
He has lightsaber duels, gets himself and his friends out of a trash compactor, and finally, drops a charge that blows up the bad guy's weapon.

[Slide 32]
The 5 Elements: Evidence
He defeats the bad guy and rescues the princess.

[Slide 33]
"A fact wrapped in a story is 22 times more memorable."
-Jerome Bruner, Cognitive Psychologist
